metadata.description: Source documents for pragma-stratified fact extraction
metadata.selection_rationale: NYT News articles are long (2000-11000 chars), factually dense, and cover diverse topics — ideal for extracting 8-10 facts per document across all pragmatic tiers (assertion, presupposition, implicature).

examples.input:
IF your cocktail passions rest with the gibson, you are all too familiar with the standard slippery cocktail onion of infinite shelf life. But at Washington Park, Jonathan Waxman's new restaurant-as-Greenmarket-showcase, a surprise garnish awaits. Standing in for those mothballesque jarred onions, you will find a group of slender, slightly pink, cheek-puckering pickled ramps perched on the lip of your glass, reaching into the drink like long, supple fingers. Pungent, with a sweet and peppery kick, they add the right tang to the classic cocktail.
The chef, Daniel Eardley, gathered the ramps, or wild leeks, this spring at an upstate farm. To achieve a balance of sweetness and acidity, he pickled them in a hot brine made from champagne vinegar, coriander, mustard seed, bay leaves, peppercorns, sugar, salt and maple sugar. ''The maple sugar is the kind of flavor that you can't put your finger on,'' Mr. Eardley said, ''but it is the key to making sure the ramps are not too assertive.''
At Washington Park, Jonathan Waxman's new restaurant-as-Greenmarket-showcase, a surprise garnish awaits.
IF your cocktail passions rest with the gibson, you are all too familiar with the standard slippery cocktail onion of infinite shelf life. But at Washington Park, Jonathan Waxman's new restaurant-as-Greenmarket-showcase, a surprise garnish awaits. Standing in for those mothballesque jarred onions, you will find a group of slender, slightly pink, cheek-puckering pickled ramps perched on the lip of your glass, reaching into the drink like long, supple fingers. Pungent, with a sweet and peppery kick, they add the right tang to the classic cocktail.
The chef, Daniel Eardley, gathered the ramps, or wild leeks, this spring at an upstate farm. To achieve a balance of sweetness and acidity, he pickled them in a hot brine made from champagne vinegar, coriander, mustard seed, bay leaves, peppercorns, sugar, salt and maple sugar. ''The maple sugar is the kind of flavor that you can't put your finger on,'' Mr. Eardley said, ''but it is the key to making sure the ramps are not too assertive.''
The ramps moved from the stockpot to the rim of the glass at the request of Alexander Miranda, the restaurant's sommelier and bar manager, who said that he was not about to serve drinks with ''horrible jarred garnishes kept in creepy containers.''
''Jonathan and Daniel go through so much trouble to put the best produce on the plate,'' Mr. Miranda said. ''That dedication should not stop at the bar.''
What's next in the line of Greenmarket garnishes? Mr. Waxman's thoughts are wandering all over the garden. ''Okra is going to be in season soon, and I think we'll pickle some and use it to garnish our jalapeño-infused bloody mary,'' he said. Just keep it away from the cosmopolitans.
ANDREA STRONG
A gibson garnished with pickled ramps is $10 at Washington Park, 24 Fifth Avenue, (Ninth Street); (212) 529-4400.
TEMPTATION

A lot of children in Hawaii are wishing for the same thing this holiday: a Christmas tree.
A severe shortage has turned the normally placid ritual of selecting a holiday pine into a frenzy of driving, phone calling and even surfing the Internet, where some desperate islanders spent nearly $300 to have their fresh-smelling bit of cheer flown in from the mainland. Live trees ran out about two weeks ago. And a few days before Christmas, even artificial trees were hard to come by.
A severe shortage has turned the normally placid ritual of selecting a holiday pine into a frenzy of driving, phone calling and even surfing the Internet.
A lot of children in Hawaii are wishing for the same thing this holiday: a Christmas tree.
A severe shortage has turned the normally placid ritual of selecting a holiday pine into a frenzy of driving, phone calling and even surfing the Internet, where some desperate islanders spent nearly $300 to have their fresh-smelling bit of cheer flown in from the mainland. Live trees ran out about two weeks ago. And a few days before Christmas, even artificial trees were hard to come by.
''I was telling my wife, 'I'll buy it next week, next week,''' said Reza Azeri, who was viewing the only artificial tree left at a Sears store in Honolulu, but could not stomach its $150 price. ''I'm in trouble.''
Maybe it is an overcompensation for the impossibility of a white Christmas, but Hawaii takes the holiday seriously. Homes are elaborately decorated, and newspapers often list the best ones to drive by. Many people buy their trees on Thanksgiving weekend. And almost every year, there is a rumor of an impending shortage. This year it actually happened.
The shortage, some vendors say, is a reaction to a glut last year, which left trees sitting on lots Christmas Day.
''Everybody got stung last year,'' said Mele Turner, an independent tree salesman who said he brought in 20 percent fewer trees than last year for his three Honolulu lots. ''That's the main reason there's such an extreme shortage.''
Mr. Turner flew in two emergency shipments of trees over the weekend, totaling 220. He said he heard that some people camped overnight for the first shipment of 130, which sold out in two hours, despite prices of $165 to $185. Early in the week, 30 trees were left.
Spooked by the competitive power of big-box stores like the Home Depot and Wal-Mart, some smaller vendors may have ordered fewer trees, and at least one dropped out entirely, said Greg Rondeau, sales manager at Holiday Tree Farms in Corvallis, Ore., which supplies the Home Depot and independent vendors in Hawaii. Mr. Rondeau said he shipped slightly more trees to Hawaii this year. But the Wal-Mart in the central Oahu town of Mililani ordered only 2,000 trees this year, half as many as last year, and sold out by mid-December, said the store's assistant manager, Kaesho Scudder. The store also ran through at least 3,000 artificial trees, Mr. Scudder said.
The Christmas department at Sears was stripped nearly bare Tuesday afternoon, except for tree skirts and boxes of ornaments, stacked into mountains despite being marked 50 percent off.
The shortage has sparked both creativity and desperation.
Mr. Azeri, who said his wife was actually very understanding, planned to hang the family ornaments on a $9 spiral of lights he bought at Ace Hardware. The home décor store Illuminations sold out of its five-foot-tall iron votive trees -- Christmas-tree-shaped candelabra -- about two weeks ago, s
examples.output: With Christmas Trees in Short Supply, Hawaiians Improvise
examples.metadata_title: With Christmas Trees in Short Supply, Hawaiians Improvise

examples.input:
The Rev. Al Sharpton has run for office three times from New York, and has lost each time. He has been embroiled in polarizing disputes throughout his career, including the handling of his personal finances and his involvement in a racially charged rape case that a jury found to be a hoax. Even the ever-energetic and indefatigable Mr. Sharpton acknowledges it is unlikely that he will ever be elected president of the United States.
Not to say Mr. Sharpton is not going to try.
Even the Rev. Al Sharpton acknowledges it is unlikely that he will ever be elected president of the United States. Not to say he is not going to try.
The Rev. Al Sharpton has run for office three times from New York, and has lost each time. He has been embroiled in polarizing disputes throughout his career, including the handling of his personal finances and his involvement in a racially charged rape case that a jury found to be a hoax. Even the ever-energetic and indefatigable Mr. Sharpton acknowledges it is unlikely that he will ever be elected president of the United States.
Not to say Mr. Sharpton is not going to try.
At 48, Mr. Sharpton is joining the field of Democratic candidates for president. He is planning to create a presidential exploratory committee on Jan. 21, and is already delivering speeches across the nation, preparing to open an office in Washington and offering opinions on everything from North Korea to deficit spending.
''I'm very serious about this,'' Mr. Sharpton said between cellphone calls over breakfast at the Four Seasons in the Georgetown section of Washington the other day. ''I'm not just talking about Sharpton getting a position in the White House. I'm talking about fighting for the soul of the Democratic Party.''
Aides in competing presidential camps describe Mr. Sharpton's candidacy as little more than a distraction, a quest on the fringe that will ultimately have little bearing on the Democratic presidential competition.
Yet Democrats acquainted with Mr. Sharpton's style and skills suggest that might be a miscalculation. Many Democrats say he could prove to be a constant and potentially unwelcome complication for the other Democrats seeking to challenge President Bush.
For two decades, Mr. Sharpton has shown an unflagging talent for winning attention for himself and his causes. (He was on the NBC News program ''Meet the Press'' for 17 minutes this morning.) His wit, bluster and bravado make him endlessly quotable, bolstering his reputation as a ruthless political street fighter.
As the only black candidate in a crowded field, Mr. Sharpton said, he would do well in states with large black populations. In particular, he said, he might win the important South Carolina Democratic primary, an assertion that did not seem outlandish to other Democrats, who noted that 40 percent of the South Carolina Democratic electorate is black.
''He clearly has some potential to get a significant number of delegates, especially in the Southern states,'' said David Bositis, senior policy analyst with the Joint Center for Political and Economic Studies, a research group that deals with African-American issues.
Mr. Sharpton is putting the others on the spot by raising issues they have avoided: calling for all Confederate flags to be taken down in South Carolina and Mississippi, for example.
He tears into the credentials of his rivals with all the enthusiasm of a Republican, calling for the ouster of Terry McAuliffe, the Democratic Party chairman, asserting
examples.output: A Wily Contender, But Unlikely Winner, Eyes the Presidency
examples.metadata_title: A Wily Contender, But Unlikely Winner, Eyes the Presidency
